Stage Sequence number the instruction is then copied from the memory location contained in the MAR (memory address register) and is placed in the MDR (memory data register) the instruction is finally decoded and is then executed the PC (program counter) contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched 1 the entire instruction is then copied from the MDR (memory data register) and placed in the CIR (current instruction register) the address contained in the PC (program counter) is copied to the MAR (memory address register) via the address bus the address part of the instruction, if any, is placed in the MAR (memory address register) the value in the PC (program counter) is then incremented so that it points to the next instruction to be fetched [6] 12 0478/11/M/J/15
mark_scheme_text: 7 (a) 2/3 matches â€“ 2 marks 1 match â€“ 1 mark [2] address bus control bus data bus this bus carries signals used to coordinate the computerâ€™s activities this bi-directional bus is used to exchange data between processor, memory and input/output devices this uni-directional bus carries signals relating to memory addresses between processor and memory Page 8 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0478 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (b) description of stage sequence number the instruction is then copied from the memory location contained in the MAR (memory address register) and is placed in the MDR (memory data register) 3 the instruction is finally decoded and is then executed 7 the PC (program counter) contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched (1) the entire instruction is then copied from the MDR (memory data register) and placed in the CIR (current instruction register) 4 the address contained in the PC (program counter) is copied to the MAR (memory address register) via the address bus 2 the address part of the instruction is placed in the MAR (memory address register) 6 the value in the PC (program counter) is then incremented so that it points to the next instruction to be fetched 5* The incrementation of the program counter can appear at any stage after 2. All other stages must be in the correct given order. [6]

Bus Description address bus this bus carries signals used to coordinate the computerâ€™s activities control bus this bi-directional bus is used to exchange data between processor, memory and input/ output devices data bus this uni-directional bus carries signals relating to memory addresses between processor and memory [2] 11 0478/13/M/J/15 [Turn over (b) The seven stages in a von Neumann fetch-execute cycle are shown in the table below. Stage Sequence number the instruction is then copied from the memory location contained in the MAR (memory address register) and is placed in the MDR (memory data register) the instruction is finally decoded and is then executed the PC (program counter) contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched 1 the entire instruction is then copied from the MDR (memory data register) and placed in the CIR (current instruction register) the address contained in the PC (program counter) is copied to the MAR (memory address register) via the address bus the address part of the instruction, if any, is placed in the MAR (memory address register) the value in the PC (program counter) is then incremented so that it points to the next instruction to be fetched [6] 12 0478/13/M/J/15
mark_scheme_text: 7 (a) 2/3 matches â€“ 2 marks 1 match â€“ 1 mark [2] address bus control bus data bus this bus carries signals used to coordinate the computerâ€™s activities this bi-directional bus is used to exchange data between processor, memory and input/output devices this uni-directional bus carries signals relating to memory addresses between processor and memory Page 8 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0478 13 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (b) description of stage sequence number the instruction is then copied from the memory location contained in the MAR (memory address register) and is placed in the MDR (memory data register) 3 the instruction is finally decoded and is then executed 7 the PC (program counter) contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched (1) the entire instruction is then copied from the MDR (memory data register) and placed in the CIR (current instruction register) 4 the address contained in the PC (program counter) is copied to the MAR (memory address register) via the address bus 2 the address part of the instruction is placed in the MAR (memory address register) 6 the value in the PC (program counter) is then incremented so that it points to the next instruction to be fetched 5* The incrementation of the program counter can appear at any stage after 2. All other stages must be in the correct given order. [6]

Steps in the printing process Step order As the printing drum rotates, a laser scans across it; this removes the positive charge in certain areas 4 The printing drum is coated in positively-charged toner; this then sticks to the negatively-charged parts of the printing drum 6 The paper goes through a fuser which melts the toner so it fixes permanently to the paper 9 The printer driver ensures that the data is in a format that the laser printer can understand (1) A negatively-charged sheet of paper is then rolled over the printing drum 7 Data is then sent to the laser printer and stored temporarily in the printer buffer 2 The toner on the printing drum is now transferred to the paper to reproduce the required text and images 8 The printing drum is given a positive charge 3 Negatively-charged areas are then produced on the printing drum; these match exactly with the text and images to be printed 5 [8] Page 9 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ October/November 2015 0478 12 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015

subject: Computer Science
question_text: Is light reading <= 50? Sensors and a microprocessor monitor a car exhaust for high temperature and high carbon monoxide (CO) levels. (a) Describe how the sensors and microprocessor are used to monitor the temperature and CO levels and warn the driver if either is out of range. [5] (b) The information from seven sensors is sent to an engine management system in the car. The status of each sensor is stored in an 8-bit register; a value of 1 indicates a fault condition: parity bit CO level too high temperature too high fuel pressure too low oil pressure too low brake pads too thin voltage too low airbag fault For example, a register showing 0 1 0 1 1 0 0 0 indicates: â€¢ temperature too high â€¢ fuel pressure too low â€¢ voltage too low 5 0478/13/O/N/15 [Turn over (i) Identify the fault condition(s) that the following register indicates: 0 0 1 0 0 1 0 1 [2] (ii) The system uses odd parity. Write the correct parity bit in each register. 1 1 1 0 0 1 0 0 0 0 1 1 1 0 [2] (iii) A car has a faulty airbag and the CO level is too high. Write what should be contained in the 8-bit register. [2] (iv) Give the hexadecimal value of the binary number shown in part (iii) . [1] 6 0478/13/O/N/15
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Any five from: â€“ sensors send signals / data to microprocessor â€“ signal / data converted to digital (by an ADC) â€“ microprocessor compares temperature / carbon monoxide level / value with stored level / value â€“ if CO level > stored value, microprocessor sends signalâ€¦ â€“ if temperature > stored value, microprocessor sends signalâ€¦ â€“ â€¦to light warning bulb on dashboard / sounds alarm [5] (b) (i) 2 marks for all correct conditions, 1 mark for 2 correct conditions CO (carbon monoxide) level too high oil pressure too low brake pads too thin [2] (ii) 1 mark for each correct parity bit in position 1 1 1 1 1 0 0 1 0 0 0 0 0 1 1 1 0 [2] (iii) 1 mark for correct parity bit + 1 mark for remainder of binary value 1 0 1 0 0 0 1 0 [2] (iv) A 2 (allow follow through from part (iii)) [1]

Pre-release Material The manager of a parcel delivery service needs a program to check the size and weight of parcels to determine which parcels can be accepted for delivery. Write and test a program for the manager. â€¢ Your program must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. You will need to complete these three tasks. Each task must be fully tested. TASK 1 â€“ Check the size and weight of a single parcel Each parcel must obey the following rules to be accepted for delivery: â€¢ each dimension must be no more than 80 cm â€¢ the sum of the three dimensions must be no more than 200 cm â€¢ the weight of the parcel must be between one and ten kilograms inclusive Input and store the weight and dimensions for one parcel. All the dimensions and the weight must be validated on entry and an unsuitable parcel rejected. Output if the parcel is accepted or rejected. If rejected, output all the reasons why the parcel was rejected. TASK 2 â€“ Check a customerâ€™s consignment of parcels Input and store the number of parcels in the consignment. Calculate the number of parcels accepted and the total weight of the parcels accepted. For each parcel that was rejected, output all the reasons why that parcel was rejected. Output the number of parcels accepted and the total weight of parcels accepted. Output the number of parcels rejected. 5(a) 1 mark per correct tick Received byte corrupted during transmission ( 9 ) not corrupted during transmission ( 9 ) 10110100 9 01101101 9 10000001 9 3

5(b) Four from: Â• Uses acknowledgement and time out Â• Check performed on received data // error is detected by e.g. parity check, check sum Â• If error detected, request sent to resend data // negative acknowledgment is used Â• If no acknowledgement is sent that data is received // positive acknowledgement is used Â• Data is resent / Resend request repeated, till data is resent correctly Â« Â• Â« or request times out // limit is reached 4 Question Answer Marks 6 1 mark for correct bus name and up to 2 further marks for appropriate purpose. Address (bus) Two from: Â• Carries / transports an address / location Â« Â• Â« of the next item to be fetched Â• Data travels one way (unidirectional) Data (bus) Two from: Â• Carries / transports data / example of data ... question_text: The processes in a chemical factory are monitored by sensors connected to a microprocessor. (a) Identify two different sensors used in this application. Give an example of how each sensor could be used in the chemical factory. Sensor
mark_scheme_text:
12(a) 1 mark for appropriate sensor and 1 further mark for appropriate use. question_text: Optical storage media can be used to store data. Describe how the data is read from a Compact Disc (CD). [4] (b) Kamil wants to store a 16-bit colour image file. The image size is 1000 pixels. Calculate the size of the file. Give your answer in bytes . Show your working. Working Answer bytes [2] (c) Describe the differences between primary and secondary storage. [4] 11 0478/11/O/N/17
mark_scheme_text:
9(a) Any four from: â€“ (Red) laser is used â€“ (Laser beams) shines onto surface of the disk â€“ It is rotated (at a constant speed) to be read â€“ Surface is covered in a track (that spirals from the centre) â€“ Data is represented on the surface using pits and lands â€“ Pits and lands represent binary values â€“ Pits reflect light back differently (to the area in between/land) â€“ Optical device can determine the binary value from the light reflection 4

9(b) 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for correct answer: â€“ 1000 â‹… 16 â€“ 16000/8 â€“ Answer is 2000 bytes 2

9(c) Four from: (Max 2 for either primary or secondary) â€“ Primary RAM and ROM â€“ Secondary HDD and SSD â€“ Primary is directly accessible by CPU â€“ Secondary is not directly accessible by CPU â€“ Primary is internal to computer â€“ Secondary can be internal or external to the computer â€“ Primary stores boot up instructions and can hold data whilst being processed â€“ Secondary stores files/software â€“ Primary has faster access speed â€“ Secondary has a slower access speed â€“ Primary has both volatile and non-volatile â€“ Secondary is non-volatile 4 0478/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 10 of 10 Question Answer Marks 10 1 mark for each correct tick Statement true ( 9 ) false ( 9 ) Assembly language uses mnemonic codes. 9 Assembly language programs do not need a translator to be executed. 9 Assembly language is a low-level programming language. 9 Assembly language is specific to the computer hardware. 9 Assembly language is machine code. 9 Assembly language is often used to create drivers for hardware. 9 6

[4] (b) Explain the process of converting the binary number 1010 into a denary number. [5] 4 0478/12/O/N/17
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Any four from ( Max 2 per number system) : Â• A binary number system is a base-2 system Â• A denary number system is a base-10 system Â• A binary number system uses 0 and 1 values Â• A denary number system uses 0 to 9 values Â• A binary number system has units/ placeholders/column headings that increase by the power of 2 Â• A denary number system has units/ placeholders/column headings that increase by the power of 10 Â• Binary has more digit for the same value// Denary has less digits for the same value 4 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 3 of 9 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Five from: Â• Correct column headings / place holders by example Â• Correctly place a 1 or a 0 for each column Â• Identify the columns to be added Â• Add together the (denary) values identified Â« Â• Â« this will give a total which is the denary number/answer Â• Answer is 10 5 Question Answer Marks

subject: Computer Science
question_text: A file server is used as a central data store for a network of computers. Rory sends data from his computer to a file server that is approximately 100 metres away. It is important that the data is transmitted accurately. Rory needs to be able to read data from and write data to the file server at the same time. (a) (i) Use ticks ( 3 ) to identify the most suitable data transmission methods for this application. Method
mark_scheme_text:
4(a)(i) Method 1 Tick ( 9 ) Method 2 Tick ( 9 ) Serial 9 Simplex Parallel Half-duplex Duplex 9 2

4(a)(ii) Any four from ( Max 3 for serial): Â• Serial has less/lower interference Â• Serial is (more) reliable/accurate over distances Â• In serial the bits wonâ€™t be skewed Â• In serial it is easier to collate the bits together again after transmission Â• Duplex transmits data in both directions at the same time Â• simplex/half-duplex/remaining methods wonâ€™t allow read and write at same time 4 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 4 of 9 Question Answer Marks

4(b) 1 mark for error checking method, 2 marks for description: Checksum Â• A value is calculated from the data // Description of calculation Â• Value is transmitted with data Â• Value is recalculated after transmission Â• If the values match the data is (more likely to be) accurate Parity check Â• A parity bit is transmitted with each byte of data Â• Odd or even (parity can be used) Â• Counts / checks number of 1â€™s // counts / checks to see if 1â€™s are even // counts / checks to see if 1â€™s are odd Â• (Each byte is) checked after transmission to see if it matches the odd/even parity used Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ) Â• Uses acknowledgement and timeout Â• When a device detects an error in data transmission it asks for the packet to be resent / no error detected, positive acknowledgment sent Â• The sending device resends the packet after the request to resend/ timeout received Â• This process is continuous until the packet received is correct/until the ARQ limit is reached Echo (check) Â• Copy of data is sent back to sender Â• Data is compared to see if it matches Â• If it does not match error detected 6 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 5 of 9 Question Answer Marks

mark_scheme_text:
8(a) Any four from: âˆ’ Shines light / (red) laser at barcode âˆ’ Light is called an illuminator âˆ’ Light is reflected back // White lines reflect light // Black lines reflect less light/absorbs light âˆ’ Sensors / photoelectric cells detect the light âˆ’ Different reflections / bars will give different binary values / digital values // pattern converted to digital values âˆ’ A microprocessor interprets the data 4

8(b) Any three from: âˆ’ barcode identifies a (unique) product âˆ’ barcode can be used to look up product (in a database) âˆ’ data about stock levels can be stored on a system âˆ’ stock can be automatically deducted from the system âˆ’ can check stock is below a certain level // check stock level âˆ’ automatic re-order // Alerts when stock is low âˆ’ automatically update new stock level âˆ’ to locate if an item of stock is available in another location 3 0478/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 10 of 12 Question Answer Marks

8(d) Secondary Storage â€“ any two from: âˆ’ Not directly accessed by the CPU âˆ’ Non-volatile storage âˆ’ Secondary is internal to the computer/device âˆ’ An example of secondary storage would be HDD/SSD Off-line storage â€“ any two from: âˆ’ Non-volatile storage âˆ’ Off-line storage is storage that is removable from a computer/device // not internal // portable âˆ’ An example of off-line storage would be CD/DVD/USB stick/SD card/magnetic tape/ external HDD/SSD 4 Question Answer Marks 9 Any six from: âˆ’ Suitable biometric device, such as fingerprint scanner/retina/eye/iris scanner/face recognition/voice recognition/palm scanner // description of use e.g. use fingerprint on device âˆ’ Sensor (in biometric device) captures/takes data/readings (of user) âˆ’ Data/readings are converted from analogue to digital (using ADC) âˆ’ Data/reading sent to the microprocessor âˆ’ Data/readings compared to stored values/data Â« âˆ’ Â« if data/readings match user can enter âˆ’ Â« if data/readings do not match user is declined entry // user asked to try again Â« âˆ’ Â« alert may be sent to security // alarm may sound 6 0478/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 11 of 12 Question Answer Marks

Use the pre-release material and your experience from attempting the tasks before the examination to answer Question 1. Pre-release material A farmer records the milk production of a herd of cows. Every cow has a unique 3-digit identity code. Each cow can be milked twice a day, seven days a week. The volume of milk from each cow is recorded in litres correct to one decimal place (yield) every time the cow is milked. The size of the herd is fixed. At the end of the week the total and the average yield for each cow for that week is calculated. The farmer identifies the cow that has produced the most milk that week. The farmer also identifies any cows that have produced less than 12 litres of milk on four or more days that week. A program is required to record the yield for each cow every time it is milked, calculate the total weekly volume of milk for the herd and the average yield per cow in a week. The program must also identify the cow with the best yield that week and identify any cows with a yield of less than 12 litres of milk for four or more days that week. Write and test a program or programs for the farmer. â€¢ Your program or programs must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly and understandably. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. You will need to complete these three tasks. Each task must be fully tested. TASK 1 â€“ Record the yield. Write a program for TASK 1 to record the milk yields for a week. The program records and stores the identity code number and the yield every time a cow is milked

subject: Computer Science
question_text: Computers can transmit data using different methods. Describe the three data transmission methods given. (i) Serial data transmission [2] (ii) Parallel data transmission [2] (iii) Duplex data transmission [2] 10 0478/12/O/N/18 (b) Data can sometimes be corrupted when it is transmitted from one computer to another, causing errors to be present in the data. Identify and describe three methods of error detection that could be used to see if an error has occurred. Error detection method
mark_scheme_text:
9(a)(i) Two from: Â• Data is transmitted one bit at a time Â• Data is transmitted using a single wire Â• Bits arrive in order/sequence 2

9(a)(ii) Two from: Â• Data is transmitted multiple bits at a time/simultaneously Â• Data is transmitted using multiple wires Â• Bits may arrive out of sequence/skewed (and are reordered) 2

9(a)(iii) 1 mark for each: Â• Data is transmitted in both directions Â• Â« at the same time/simultaneously 2 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 12 of 12 Question Answer Marks

9(b) Maximum of three marks per error detection method. 1 mark for naming the method, 2 marks for describing it. Parity (check) Â• Odd or even parity can be used Â• Bits are added together // 1 bits are counted Â• Parity bit added (depending on parity set) Â• Parity checked on receipt Â• If parity bit is incorrect an error is detected Checksum Â• Calculation performed on data (to get the checksum) Â• Checksum sent with data Â• Checksum recalculated after transmission Â• Comparison made between checksum before and checksum after transmission Â• Error detected if checksums are different Automatic repeat request (ARQ) Â• Uses acknowledgement and timeout Â• Request is sent (with data) requiring acknowledgement Â• If no response/acknowledgment within certain time frame data package is resent Â• When data received contains an error a request is sent (automatically) to resend the data Â• The resend request is repeatedly sent until packet is received error free/limit is reached/acknowledgement received 9 Question Answer Marks 10 Five from: Â• The sensor sends data to the microprocessor Â• The analogue data is converted to digital (using ADC) Â• The microprocessor compares the reading to the set range/stored values/stored data (6 to 8) Â« â€“ Â« If the reading is >8 or <6 / outside range Â« o Â« the microprocessor sends a signal to output the alert Â• The process is continuous/repeated 5

subject: Computer Science
question_text: Problem-solving and Programming October/November 2018 1 hour 45 minutes Candidates answer on the Question Paper. No Additional Materials are required. No calculators allowed. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST Write your Centre number, candidate number and name in the spaces at the top of this page. Write in dark blue or black pen. You may use an HB pencil for any diagrams, graphs or rough working. Do not use staples, paper clips, glue or correction fluid. DO NOT WRITE IN ANY BARCODES. Answer all questions. DO NOT ATTEMPT TASKS 1, AND 3 in the pre-release material; these are for information only. You are advised to spend no more than 40 minutes on Section A (Question 1). No marks will be awarded for using brand names of software packages or hardware. Any businesses described in this paper are entirely fictitious. At the end of the examination, fasten all your work securely together. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. The maximum number of marks is 50. This syllabus is approved for use in England, Wales and Northern Ireland as a Cambridge International Level 1/Level Certificate. 2 0478/22/O/N/18 Section A You are advised to spend no longer than 40 minutes answering this section. Here is a copy of the pre-release material. DO NOT attempt Tasks 1, 2 and 3 now. Use the pre-release material and your experience from attempting the tasks before the examination to answer Question 1. Pre-release material A junior park run event is held every week on a Saturday morning in a local park over a distance of two kilometres. Children between the ages of 4 and 14 inclusive can register to take part. Children register with their name and age. When they register, they are allocated a unique identification number of four digits; the last digit is a check digit. Once registered a child can take part in junior park run events for a year. For each event, the organisers record the time each child takes to run two kilometres. Their time is stored for every event they complete and the number of runs they have completed is updated by one. If their time is faster than their personal best (PB) time, their PB time is updated. When a child has completed 11 runs, they are awarded a half-marathon wristband. When a child has completed 22 runs, they are awarded a full-marathon wristband. A program is required to update the childrenâ€™s data, update PB times if necessary, and decide if a wristband is to be awarded. Pre-release material An auction company has an interactive auction board at their sale rooms, which allows buyers to place bids at any time during the auction. Before the auction starts, the sellers place their items in the sale room with a unique number attached to each item (item number). The following details about each item need to be set up on the interactive auction board system: item number, number of bids, description and reserve price. The number of bids is initially set to zero. During the auction, buyers can look at the items in the sale room and then place a bid on the interactive auction board at the sale room. Each buyer is given a unique number for identification (buyer number). All the buyer needs to do is enter their buyer number, the item number and their bid. Their bid must be greater than any existing bids. At the end of the auction, the company checks all the items and marks those that have bids greater than the reserve as sold. Any items sold will incur a fee of 10% of the final bid to be paid to the auction company. question_text: Problem-solving and Programming May/June 2021 1 hour 45 minutes You must answer on the question paper. No additional materials are needed. INSTRUCTIONS â— Answer all questions. â— Do not attempt Tasks 1, 2 and 3 in the copy of the pre-release material on page 2; these are for information only. â— Use a black or dark blue pen. You may use an HB pencil for any diagrams or graphs. â— Write your name, centre number and candidate number in the boxes at the top of the page. â— Write your answer to each question in the space provided. â— Do not use an erasable pen or correction fluid. â— Do not write on any bar codes. â— Calculators must not be used in this paper. INFORMATION â— The total mark for this paper is 50. â— The number of marks for each question or part question is shown in brackets [ ]. â— No marks will be awarded for using brand names of software packages or hardware. * 0 3 3 7 1 8 6 9 5 9 * DC (LK) 202758/1 [Turn over 2 0478/22/M/J/21 Section A You are advised to spend no longer than 40 minutes answering this section. Here is a copy of the pre-release material. DO NOT attempt Tasks 1, 2 and 3 now. Use the pre-release material and your experience from attempting the tasks before the examination to answer Question 1. Pre-release material An electric mountain railway makes four return trips every day. In each trip the train goes up the mountain and back down. The train leaves from the foot of the mountain at 09:00, 11:00, 13:00 and 15:00. The train returns from the top of the mountain at 10:00, 12:00, 14:00 and 16:00. Each train has six coaches with eighty seats available in each coach. Passengers can only purchase a return ticket; all tickets must be purchased on the day of travel. The cost is $25 for the journey up and $25 for the journey down. Groups of between ten and eighty passengers inclusive get a free ticket for every tenth passenger, provided they all travel together (every tenth passenger travels free). Passengers must book their return train journey, as well as the departure train journey, when they purchase their ticket. Passengers can return on the next train down the mountain or a later train. The last train from the top of the mountain has two extra coaches on it. The train times are displayed on a large screen, together with the number of tickets still available for each train. Every time a ticket is booked the display is updated. When a train is full, the word â€˜Closedâ€™ is displayed instead of the number of tickets available. Write and test a program or programs for the electric mountain railway. â€¢ Your program or programs must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data; data must be validated on entry. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly and understandably. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. You will need to complete these three tasks. Each task must be fully tested. Task 1 â€“ Start of the day. Write a program to set up the screen display for the start of the day.

When a booking is made, the name of the guest is recorded together with their mobile phone number. Once the squash court is booked, the guest is shown the court number together with a unique 4-digit code that can be used to unlock the squash court. Each booking is for one squash court for one hour. The 4-digit code must be different for each booking. Write and test a program or programs for a computer system to manage the daily squash court bookings. â€¢ Your program or programs must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data; data must be validated on entry. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly and understandably. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. You will need to complete these three tasks. Each task must be fully tested. Set up a screen to display the court availability at the start of the day. Task 2 â€“ making a squash court booking Check if there is a squash court available at the time requested. If a squash court is available, record the guestâ€™s name and mobile phone number. Mark the squash court as booked for that hour. Generate and record the unique

question_text: Rebekah discovers that her bank details have been used fraudulently. She thinks her bank details were stolen electronically, whilst she was using the Internet. (a) Identify and describe two methods that could have been used to steal Rebekahâ€™s bank details electronically. Method
mark_scheme_text:

8(b) Any two from: e.g. â€¢ A MAC address is assigned by the manufacturer, whereas an IP address is assigned by the network/router/ISP â€¢ A MAC address is represented as hexadecimal, whereas an IP address can sometimes be represented as numeric â€¢ A MAC address is normally static, whereas an IP address can be dynamic â€¢ A MAC address has 6 groups of digits, whereas an IP address has 4/8 groups â€¢ A MAC address is 6 bytes (48 bit), whereas an IP address is 4/ 16 bytes (32/128 bit) 2 0478 / 13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 14 of 15 Question Answer Marks 9 Any six from (MAX four for ARQ): â€¢ Odd or even parity is set/agreed for the data â€¢ A parity bit is added to each byte of data â€¢ â€¦ to make the number of 1s match parity â€¢ Data is checked after transmission to see if parity is correct â€¢ ARQ uses acknowledgement and timeout â€¢ If no error is found, a positive acknowledgement is sent to the sender / no acknowledgement is sent to the sender â€¢ If an error is found, a negative acknowledgement is sent to the sender â€¦ â€¢ â€¦ that triggers the data to be resent â€¢ When the data is sent, a timer is started â€¢ If an acknowledgement is not received within the time set, the data is resent .. . â€¢ â€¦ until an acknowledgement is received / resend limit is reached 6 0478 / 13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 15 of 15 Question Answer Marks

8(f) Any two from: Example: ï‚· The robot can now adapt itself by changing its own rules/data/ processes ï‚· The robot will become more efficient ï‚· â€¦ as it has a greater knowledge of its surroundings / environment ï‚· â€¦ as it can remember where a fence is ï‚· â€¦ as it can remember the route it needs to take ï‚· â€¦ as it can remember where any obstacles are to avoid ï‚· â€¦ as it can remember where to start and stop sowing seeds ï‚· â€¦ as it may make fewer errors 2

INPUT Number IF Number < 3 OR Number > 20 THEN OUTPUT "The number of rooms must be between 3 and 20 inclusive, please try again" ENDIF UNTIL Number >= 3 AND Number <= 20 // input of room names and dimensions â€“ could be more than one loop FOR InLoop ï‚¬ 1 TO Number OUTPUT "Enter the name of room ", InLoop INPUT Rooms[InLoop] OUTPUT "Enter the length of the room in metres" INPUT Dimensions[InLoop, 1] OUTPUT "Enter the width of the room in metres" INPUT Dimensions[InLoop, 2] Dimensions[InLoop, 3] ï‚¬ ROUND(Dimensions[InLoop, 1] * Dimensions[InLoop, 2], 2) NEXT InLoop // calculates total area and average area â€“ rounded values have been stored TotArea ï‚¬ 0 FOR Total ï‚¬ 1 TO Number TotArea ï‚¬ TotArea + Dimensions[Total, 3] NEXT Total AvArea ï‚¬ ROUND(TotArea / Number, 2) 15 0478 / 22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 12 of 14 Question Answer Marks 12 // finding largest and smallest rooms Larea ï‚¬ 0 Sarea ï‚¬ 100000 Lindex ï‚¬ 1 Sindex ï‚¬ 1 FOR Count ï‚¬ 1 TO Number IF Dimensions[Count, 3] > Larea THEN Larea ï‚¬ Dimensions[Count, 3] Lindex ï‚¬ Count ENDIF IF Dimensions[Count, 3] < Sarea THEN Sarea ï‚¬ Dimensions[Count, 3] Sindex ï‚¬ Count ENDIF NEXT C

question_text: The one-dimensional (1D) array CompetitorName[] contains the names of 25 competitors in a tournament that includes five events. Another two-dimensional (2D) array CompetitorScore[] contains the points scored by each competitor in the five events. The first dimension of this array contains the index of the competitor from CompetitorName[] and the second dimension contains their scores. Each competitor receives a score out of 100 for each event. These scores are manually entered into CompetitorScore[] Medals are awarded to the competitor or the competitors who have: â€¢ the highest points scored in each of the five events â€¢ the highest total points scored for all five events. The one-dimensional (1D) array Points[] stores the total number of points scored for each competitor. question_text: A collector needs a video library system for a collection of videos stored on 4K Blu-ray discs, standard Blu-ray discs, DVD, or as digital downloads. The two-dimensional (2D) array Video[] is used to store, for each video item, the title, the format of the video (4K Blu-ray disc, standard Blu-ray disc, DVD, or digital download), the year it was released stored as a string and a storage code to represent where the video can be found in the collection, for example: Video[1,1] is "Macbeth" Video[1,2] is "digital download" Video[1,3] is "2015" Video[1,4] is "DG276" The two-dimensional (2D) array Results[] is used to store the results of a search to find a specific video. The search uses the video title, and if the video is found, its data is copied from the array Video[] to the array Results[]. The search continues and if other videos with the same title are found, this data is also copied to the array Results[]. The search ends when the end of the data is reached. Write a program that meets the following requirements: â€¢ The video library array needs to be initialised with the null string (""), allowing for up to 10 000 records. The array Results[] must be initialised before every search with the null string and must be able to hold up to 20 search results with the same title. â€¢ Create a menu to add a new video to the library to search for an existing video by title, or to stop, with validation of the input. â€¢ When the user enters a new video, the following data is stored in the first available location of the relevant array: o video title o format (4K Blu-ray disc, standard Blu-ray disc, DVD, digital download) o year of release as string o storage code. â€¢ Allow input of data for another video if required. â€¢ When searching for an existing video, if a match is found, transfer all the data for that video into the array Results[], and continue to search until all videos with the same title have been found. â€¢ Output the results from the array Results[] â€¢ If the video title is not found, output a suitable message. â€¢ The system returns to the menu after completing the input or the output, until the user chooses to stop. You must use pseudocode or program code and add comments to explain how your code works. You do not need to declare any arrays, variables or constants. You may assume that this has already been done. 9(a) One mark for each correct line : DECLARE Name : STRING DECLARE Age : INTEGER DECLARE Found : BOOLEAN 3

9(b)(i) One mark for each bullet point â€¢ using PROCEDURE StoreData and ENDPROCEDURE â€¢ defining the parameter correctly â€¢ correctly opening the file for write â€¢ correctly storing the name â€¢ correctly closing the file Example PROCEDURE Store Data (Name : STRING) OPENFILE People.txt FOR WRITE WRITEFILE People.txt, Name CLOSEFILE People.txt ENDPROCEDURE 5 0478 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 16 of 22 Question Answer Marks

9 (b)(ii) One mark per bullet point : â€¢ use of call â€¢ correct procedure call with â€˜Suellaâ€™ CALL StoreData("Suella") 2 10 Use the tables for A02 and A03 below to award a mark in a suitable band using a best fit approach , t hen add up the total. Marks are available for: â€¢ AO2 (maximum 9 marks) â€¢ AO3 (maximum 6 marks) Data Structures required names shown underlined must be used as given in the scenario Arrays or lists : NumberGenerated[] Requirements (techniques) R1 input and validates the names of the players (iteration, input, output) R2 generates the random numbers for each player. Calculates and store the number of points per player (iteration, totalling and selection) R3 finds and outputs the name and score of the players in order with a suitable message taking into account a draw(iteration, selection and output) 15 0478 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 17 of 22 Question Answer Marks 10 Example 15 - mark answer in pseudocode. // programming techniques of iteration, selection, totalling and output are used OUTPUT "Please enter the name of player 1 " // input and validate player 1 with INPUT Player1 // presence check W HILE Player1 = "" DO OUTPUT "You must enter a name, try again " INPUT Player1 ENDWHILE OUTPUT "Please enter the name of player 2 " // input and validate player 2 INPUT Player2 W HILE Player2 = "" DO OUTPUT "You must enter a name, try again " INPUT Player2 ENDWHILE // generating the 100 random numbers for each competitor FOR X ï‚¬ 1 TO 2 FOR Y ï‚¬ 1 TO 100 // generates 100 random number between 1 and 6 for each player NumberGenerated[X, Y] = ROUND(RANDOM() * 5, 0) + 1 NEXT Y NEXT X 0478 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 18 of 22 Question Answer Marks 10 //comparing the random numbers FOR X ï‚¬ 1 to 100 IF NumberGenerated[1, X] > NumberGenerated[2, X] THEN Points[1] ï‚¬ Points[1] + 2 ELSE IF NumberGenerated[1, X] < NumberGenerated[2, X] THEN Points[2] ï‚¬ Points[2] + 2 ENDIF ENDIF IF NumberGenerated[1, X] = NumberGenerated[2, X] // if tied (the same) THEN Points[1] ï‚¬ Points[1] + 1 Points[2] ï‚¬ Points[2] + 1 ENDIF NEXT X 0478 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 19 of 22 Question Answer Ma
